coredata.dc:title: Exciton energy transfer-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ through aptamer-programmed self-assembly of QDs
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Huan
Li, Jingshuai
Li, Yuewen
Wu, Dan
Ma, Hongmin
Wei, Qin
Du, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, an original exciton energy transfer-based sensitive fluorescence sensor for the determination of Hg2+ has been designed through DNA aptamer-programmed self-assembly of CdTe quantum dots (QDs). In this work, CdTe QDs were applied as fluorescence signal source. The two pieces of T-rich aptamer played a role as molecular recognition probes which could bind to the target Hg2+ specifically. The extent of Hg2+-triggered self-assembly of QDs depended on the concentration of Hg2+, which resulted in an exciton energy transfer effect between QDs, giving an obvious fluorescence signal decrease and red-shift of the fluorescent peak. Based on this principle, we could detect the Hg2+ in two different signal modes. The limit of detection (LOD) was 3.33 nM. The proposed sensing method exhibited its application in detecting Hg2+ in real water samples with satisfactory performance. The results indicated that this proposed sensor will be of great potential in biological and analytical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: QDs
Self-assembly
Aptamer
Exciton energy transfer
Fluorescent sensor
Hg2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in practical samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chinese ( 21377046 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR201702070162 ), the Special Project of Independent Innovation and Achievements Transformation of Shandong Province ( 2014ZZCX05101 ), the Science and Technology Development Plan Project of Shandong Province ( 2014GGH217006 ), and QW thanks the Special Foundation for Taishan Scholar Professorship of Shandong Province and UJN (No. ts20130937 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra (red line) and absorption spectra (black line) of TGA-QDs. (B) FT-IR spectra of TGA (red line) and TGA-QDs (black line). (C) HRTEM imaging of the TGA-QDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Scheme of the fluorescent sensor through aptamer-programmed self-assembly of QDs. After addition of Hg2+, the formation of T-Hg2+-T structure induces the aggregation of QDs, which accompanied by a band red-shift and significant fluorescence quenching because of the exciton energy transfer effect between assembled QDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Normalized fluorescent spectra of the QDs. (a) P1-QDs (200 nM); (b) P2-QDs (200 nM); (c) P1-QDs (100 nM) + P2-QDs (100 nM); (d) P1-QDs (200 nM) + Hg2+ (50 nM); (e) P2-QDs (200 nM) + Hg2+ (50 nM); (f) P1-QDs (100 nM) + P2-QDs (100 nM) + Hg2+ (50 nM). (B) UV–vis absorbance spectra for P1-QDs (100 nM) and P2-QDs (100 nM) complex in the absence (1) and the presence (2) of Hg2+ (50 nM). (C) TEM imaging of P1-QDs (100 nM) and P2-QDs (100 nM) complex in the presence of Hg2+ (50 nM).
Effects of (A) the cross-linking molar ratio of DNA and QDs, (B) P1/P2-QDs concentrations, (C) incubating time, and (D) pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency. Conditions: Hg2+, 50 nM.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the P1/P2-QDs (100 nM) after adding Hg2+ with different concentrations. (B) Plot of fluorescence quenching efficiency and wavelength shift as a function of Hg2+ concentration.
Selectivity of the fluorescent sensing system for Hg2+ detection. The concentration of Hg2+ and other interference metal ions are 50 nM and 200 nM, respectively.
Comparison of our work and previous reports.
Recovery of Hg2+ from tap water samples using standard addition method.

head.ce:title: Exciton energy transfer-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ through aptamer-programmed self-assembly of QDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Environment, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, an original exciton energy transfer-based sensitive fluorescence sensor for the determination of Hg2+ has been designed through DNA aptamer-programmed self-assembly of CdTe quantum dots (QDs). In this work, CdTe QDs were applied as fluorescence signal source. The two pieces of T-rich aptamer played a role as molecular recognition probes which could bind to the target Hg2+ specifically. The extent of Hg2+-triggered self-assembly of QDs depended on the concentration of Hg2+, which resulted in an exciton energy transfer effect between QDs, giving an obvious fluorescence signal decrease and red-shift of the fluorescent peak. Based on this principle, we could detect the Hg2+ in two different signal modes. The limit of detection (LOD) was 3.33 nM. The proposed sensing method exhibited its application in detecting Hg2+ in real water samples with satisfactory performance. The results indicated that this proposed sensor will be of great potential in biological and analytical fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions are listed as very dangerous pollutants. These heavy metal ions pollutants could cause serious damage to human beings and environment through bioaccumulation and direct toxicity [1,2], which come from mining, fertilizers and industrial wastes. And mercury ions, a kind of heavy metal ions, could lead to kidney disease, nervous system disease and other illnesses because of its serious toxicity even there were very little mercury ions [3,4]. The above all problems caused by Hg2+ have become an important worldwide issue in the past decades. As we know, there are many methods to detect Hg2+, such as cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectroscopy [5], atomic absorption spectroscopy [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [7], etc. The above detection methods are selective and sensitive but they always need sophisticated instruments and complicated sample preparation, which limit their application in detecting Hg2+. It's obviously necessary to design a new and efficient Hg2+ detection method.

In traditional fluorescent sensors, organic dye molecules are usually applied as fluorescent signal source [8–10]. However, organic dyes are always destroyed by photobleaching. Recent years, inorganic quantum dots (QDs) have attracted a lot of attention because QDs not only possess excellent resistance to photobleaching but also possess broad absorption spectra, narrow emission band and high Stokes shifts. What's more, it also has size-tunable emission compared to organic dyes [11,12]. QDs have been studied extensively, and they have been applied in biological imaging [13,14], biosensors [15,16], etc. To this day, some QDs-based fluorescence sensor have been established which was used to detect DNA [17,18], metal ions [19,20] and other chemicals [21,22]. However, in the previous reports, it always needed another substance, a quencher, to quench the fluorescence of QDs [23–25]. In this paper, we aim to design a fluorescent sensor based on aptamer-programmed self-assembly of QDs to achieve the detection of Hg2+. The aggregation of QDs will lead to a self-quenching effect, giving a easily detectable red shift of the fluorescent peak and fluorescence signal quenching [26], which makes us to detect Hg2+ in different signal modes. Compared to previous reported QDs-based fluorescent sensor, the fluorescent sensor didn't need another substance as a quencher to quench the fluorescence of QDs. What's more, our fluorescent sensor provided two different signal modes to detect Hg2+, which could avoid accidental errors and make the detection results more credible.

It was reported that the T-rich oligodeoxyribonucleotide could recognize mercury ions via forming an Hg2+-mediated base pair (T-Hg2+-T) [27]. In this sensing system, we designed two pieces of T-rich aptamer sequences which were attached to CdTe QDs respectively to recognize and detect Hg2+. After the addition of target Hg2+, QDs could aggregate accompanied by a red-shift of the fluorescent peak and significant fluorescent signal decreasing because of the exciton energy transfer of the assembled QDs. Based on this, we achieved the detection of Hg2+ in real samples, and the results were satisfactory. It's a fact that the fluorescence sensing strategy provides high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Hg2+. So, the fluorescent sensor is of great potential to be applied in practical application in the future.
The relevant chemicals and apparatus were listed in Supporting Information.

According to a method described in the previous literature, we prepared thioglycolic acid (TGA)-capped CdTe QDs with minor modification [28]. In brief, 0.985 g (2.35 mmol) Cd(ClO4)2·6H2O was dissolved in 125 mL ultrapure water, and then 5.7 mmol of the TGA was added in the state of stirring. After that, we used 2 M NaOH to adjust the pH of the mixture to 11.2–11.5. Then this solution was poured into a 250 mL round bottom flask and was deaerated by N2 bubbling for ∼30 min. Meanwhile, 0.1 g telluride powers, 3 mL ultrapure water and 0.25 g NaBH4 were added sequentially into another 50 mL round bottom flask. After telluride powers were dissolved gradually, 10 mL H2SO4 solution (4 M) was added swiftly to generate H2Te gas, and the generated gas was pressed to the Cd solution with N2 flow under stirring. Finally, this mixture was heated to 100 °C and refluxed for about 30 h. Then, TGA-CdTe QDs could be obtained, which had red fluorescence. The obtained QDs was subjected to dialysis against the ultrapure water (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) to purify QDs. Afterwards, the QDs solution was freeze-dried. Then the QDs were redissolved in dilution buffer solution and stored at 4 °C. The mole concentration of CdTe QDs was determined as a procedure reported by Peng et al. [29].

The CdTe QDs and aptamer P1 were connected with EDC-NHS coupling agents. Specifically, the as-prepared TGA-CdTe QDs were first incubated with NHS and EDC (QDs/NHS/EDC = 1:150:100 in molar ratio) for 20 min. Then, the aptamer P1 solution was added and reacted for 150 min. The excessive unreacted substance was separated by dialysis. The aptamer P1 labeled QDs (P1-QDs) were stored at 4 °C. Modification of QDs with aptamer P2 was conducted with the same steps, and the corresponding labeled CdTe QDs were named as P2-QDs.

For Hg2+ determination, identical P1-QDs and P2-QDs were simultaneously added into reaction buffer solution, and subsequently, Hg2+ with different concentrations were added. We measured the fluorescence spectra of the solution at room temperature after reacting for 60 min, and the excitation wavelength was set as 370 nm. To evaluating the target-specificity of the fluorescent sensor, the assays for different metal ions (Zn2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, and Ag+) detection were conducted. According to the equation: I = (F0 F)/F0, we could calculate the fluorescence quenching efficiency, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of QDs in the presence and absence of target Hg2+ respectively. Meanwhile, a red shift of fluorescent emission peaks could be observed with the increase of target concentration.

The proposed fluorescent sensing system was used to analyze Hg2+ in tap water which was obtained from the tap in our laboratory. These assays were performed similar to detecting Hg2+ in buffer solution.

The fluorescence emission spectra and absorption spectra of the TGA-CdTe QDs were shown in Fig. 1 A. The peak of the first exciton absorption transition was located at 560 nm, and the fluorescent peak moved to 617 nm, giving a Stokes-shifted with 57 nm. As was shown in Fig. 1B, the peaks at 3134, 1590 and 1397 cm−1 proved the successful preparation of TGA-QDs. The HRTEM characterization is important for nanomaterials [30–32]. HRTEM image of TGA-QDs was exhibited in Fig. 1C, and their diameter was calculated to be about 3.34 nm (Part Ⅱ, Supporting Information).

The sensing mechanism of the fluorescence sensing system was shown in Fig. 2 which was based on target-induced self-quenching of QDs. Herein, we chose two pieces of T-rich aptamer sequences to recognize Hg2+. First, TGA-CdTe QDs were prepared which was used to output fluorescent signal. P1 and P2, using as recognition probes, were then modified on the surface of different QDs, respectively. Identical P1-QDs and P2-QDs were then added into reaction buffer solution simultaneously. The aptamer on the surface of QDs was in random coil conformation in the absence of Hg2+. After adding Hg2+ into the above solution, it would form stable sandwich-format complex with Hg2+ due to the formation of T-Hg2+-T structure. So target Hg2+ could glue P1 and P2 together, and the QDs would form aggregates subsequently (see Fig. 3 C), which accompanied by a band red-shift and significant fluorescent signal decreasing because of the exciton energy transfer effect between assembled QDs. Based on this, the target Hg2+ could be recognized and detected quantitatively.

To prove the feasibility of the sensing mechanism, a series of control experiments were further studied (see Fig. 3A). First, when only P1-QDs or P2-QDs was used to detect Hg2+ (curve d and e in Fig. 3A), no obvious fluorescence intensity changed compared to that of only P1-QDs, P2-QDs or P1-QDs and P2-QDs in solution (curve a, b or c in Fig. 3A). Both of the P1-QDs and P2-QDs were used to detect Hg2+ (curve f in Fig. 3A), the fluorescent signal was quenched and the fluorescence emission peak was red-shift. The results clearly suggested that the sensing mechanism of the fluorescent sensor was feasible.

There existed two theories to clarify the fluorescence decreasing of QDs when QDs aggregated: electronic coupling and exciton energy transfer [33–35]. In the former mechanism, it would be happened that hybridization of the band edge orbital of CdTe QDs due to the strong electronic coupling between neighboring QDs, which not only induced fluorescence quenching but also resulted in a red-shift in both emission spectra and absorption spectra. The second theory suggested that exciton energy of QDs with higher bandgap could migrate to QDs with smaller bandgap, which could lead to fluorescent signal decreasing, accompanied by only red-shift of fluorescent peak. In order to confirm the mechanism of fluorescent signal decreasing in this sensing system, we recorded their absorbance spectra. As exhibited in Fig. 3B, the absorbance spectra of aggregated QDs showed no obvious change compared to that of free QDs, which suggested possible exciton energy transfer between interparticles.

To get the best sensing performance, the detection conditions were optimized before detecting Hg2+ using this proposed fluorescent sensor, including the cross-linking molar ratio of DNA and QDs, QDs concentrations, incubating time and pH value of the system.

We explored the cross-linking molar ratio of DNA and QDs in the process of preparing P1/P2-QDs. The experimental data was exhibited in Fig. 4 A. We could see that the optimum fluorescence quenching efficiency appeared when the cross-linking molar ratio of DNA and QDs was 16. The molar ratio of 16 (nDNA: nQDs) was chosen in our experiment. The concentration of P1/P2-QDs is very important to our fluorescent sensing system. From Fig. 4B, we could see that the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased with the increasing concentration of P1/P2-QDs up to 100 nM. When the concentration of P1/P2-QDs was more than 100 nM, the background signal increased, which would cause the decrease of fluorescence quenching efficiency.

The incubating time and pH value are also vital in the sensing system [36–40]. It's obvious in Fig. 4C that the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased with the increasing of incubating time and then appeared an equilibrium at 60 min. Based on this, 60 min was chosen as the optimal incubating time. Finally, the pH value of the fluorescence sensing system was also explored. As shown in Fig. 4D, therefore, the following experiments were conducted in the buffer solution which pH value was 7.5.

Under the optimized detection conditions, we studied the fluorescence emission spectra of our sensing system after adding Hg2+ with different concentrations (Fig. 5 ). As exhibited in Fig. 5A, the target-induced self-quenching of QDs-based fluorescent sensing system enables us to detect Hg2+ quantitatively. The change of fluorescent signal was very sensitive to Hg2+ concentration. With increasing Hg2+ concentration, the fluorescence emission spectra exhibited a gradual fluorescence quenching and a red shift of fluorescence emission wavelength. This is because that Hg2+ could trigger the aggregation of QDs. We could obtain a good linear relationship ((F0-F)/F0 = 0.00277c + 0.01085, R2 = 0.991) between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and Hg2+ concentration over the range of 0–100 nM (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). The limit of detection of 3.33 nM (on the basis of 3 times signal-to-background ratio) could be obtained, and the limit of detection was lower than 10 nM which was permitted by most countries. The lower limit of detection may be given the credit to the specificity of Hg2+-specific aptamers.

Meanwhile, there was a band red-shift of the fluorescence emission spectra with the increasing of Hg2+ concentration. What's more, the red-shift of fluorescence emission spectra induced by Hg2+ as a function of Hg2+ concentration had the similar trend with that of the Hg2+-induced fluorescence decreasing (Fig. 5B). Therefore, this result could allow us detect Hg2+ in two different models. We also compared the performance of the designed fluorescent sensor with previously reported Hg2+ sensors including colorimetric, electrochemical and fluorescent sensors (see Table 1 ). It indicated that this sensing method was more sensitive than the majority of Hg2+ sensor.

The selectivity of our fluorescent sensing system was also explored [41,42]. We monitored the change of fluorescence intensity when the fluorescent sensor was challenged with other metal ions, including Zn2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ag+ and their mixture. As exhibited in Fig. 6 , the results suggested that the sensing system had an excellent target-specificity. Moreover, Hg2+ still could be detected specifically even in the presence of the above metal ions together. As everyone knows, selectivity is a very important parameter for the fluorescent sensor. The high selectivity of our fluorescent sensing system is based on the fact that T-T mismatch base pairs have high affinity for Hg2+ compared with many other metal ions [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the practicability of our sensing system, we used standard addition method to detect Hg2+ content in tap water. First, we adopted the proposed fluorescent sensor to determinate Hg2+ content in practical samples. According to our method, the Hg2+ concentration in tap water could be calculated as 3.8 nM. The samples were then spiked with standard Hg2+ solutions (30, 40 and 50 nM) before detecting Hg2+ concentration using this fluorescent method. Every sample was detected five times and the results were exhibited in Table 2 . These results showed that our fluorescent sensing system had the potential for detecting Hg2+ in real samples.

In summary, a novel fluorescent sensor based on Hg2+-induced aggregation of functional QDs was designed. The signal transduction mechanism of the assembled QDs was due to the exciton energy transfer. For this mechanism, exciton energy of QDs with higher bandgap could migrate to QDs with smaller bandgap, which could lead to fluorescent signal decreasing, accompanied by a red-shift of fluorescent peak. The sensing method gave a LOD as low as 3.33 nM. Moreover, our fluorescent sensor could detect Hg2+ specifically even in the presence of other metal ions. Therefore, our fluorescent sensing system for Hg2+ determination was simple and effective. Undoubtedly, the exciton energy transfer-based fluorescent sensor through target-induced aggregation of functional QDs would offer great potential for detection of other metal ions in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chinese (21377046), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR201702070162), the Special Project of Independent Innovation and Achievements Transformation of Shandong Province (2014ZZCX05101), the Science and Technology Development Plan Project of Shandong Province (2014GGH217006), and QW thanks the Special Foundation for Taishan Scholar Professorship of Shandong Province and UJN (No. ts20130937).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.10.021.
